prompt:
The smart-home hub firmware needs its automation dispatcher rewritten. Today every automation gets every event as a raw Object and instanceof-ladders its way out — and the moment an automation reacts to an event by raising another one (motion → publish DoorOpened → some automation unsubscribes itself) we get ConcurrentModificationExceptions or, worse, automations firing for events published before they existed. I want a small, typed, re-entrancy-safe dispatcher. Single-threaded, deterministic, JDK only. Two sibling .java files (no packages): Subscription.java and HubBus.java.

Subscription.java — public interface Subscription { boolean cancel(); boolean active(); } cancel() deactivates and returns true the first time, false ever after; active() reports it.

HubBus.java — public final class HubBus with:
- public <E> Subscription subscribe(Class<E> type, int priority, Consumer<? super E> handler)
- public <E> Subscription subscribeOnce(Class<E> type, int priority, Consumer<? super E> handler)
- public int publish(Object event) — returns how many handlers this call actually invoked (nested publishes count for themselves, not the outer call)
- public int listenerCount(Class<?> type) — ACTIVE subscriptions registered with exactly that Class token (not assignability)
Null type/handler/event arguments: IllegalArgumentException with message exactly "type must not be null" / "handler must not be null" / "event must not be null".

Dispatch semantics — these are the contract, the tests pin every one:
1. A subscription for token T receives event e iff T.isInstance(e): a HubEvent.class listener sees every HubEvent subtype, an Object.class listener sees everything. Note the handler is Consumer<? super E>, so a Consumer<Object> can be registered under a specific token.
2. Order per publish: higher priority number first; equal priority falls back to registration order (bus-wide registration sequence, regardless of which token they registered under).
3. Re-entrancy, the whole point: publish operates on a snapshot of the matching subscriptions taken when it starts. A listener registered DURING dispatch never sees the in-flight event (it gets the next one). A snapshotted listener that gets cancelled before its turn is skipped and NOT counted. A listener may cancel itself freely.
4. Once-listeners are deactivated immediately BEFORE their handler runs: inside its own handler active() is already false, a recursive publish of the same event type cannot re-enter it, and it counts toward the invoked total exactly once. A once-listener cancelled before it ever fires just never fires.
5. A nested publish from inside a handler dispatches depth-first — it completes fully (its listeners run) before the outer dispatch moves to its next listener.
6. If a handler throws, the exception propagates AS-IS out of publish immediately; remaining snapshotted listeners are not invoked; the bus must remain fully usable afterwards; a once-listener that threw is still consumed.

TestMain.java in this directory is the acceptance suite and pins ordering via log lists, the re-entrant cases, the once/exception interactions, and the exact IllegalArgumentException messages. Don't edit it. Done = `java TestMain.java` prints all PASS, exit 0.
